article.title: MIT Maps the 3D Atomic Structure of Relaxor Ferroelectrics for the First Time, Revealing Polar Regions Smaller Than Models Predicted
article.summary: A team led by MIT used multi-slice electron ptychography to image the inner structure of a PMN-PT relaxor for the first time, finding polar regions smaller and more intricate than leading simulations expected.
article.body_markdown:
## Overview

Researchers led by MIT have, for the first time, directly imaged the three-dimensional atomic structure of a relaxor ferroelectric, a class of materials that has powered medical ultrasounds, sonar systems, and high-precision sensors for decades despite long-standing uncertainty about how it works at the atomic scale. The team reported the result on April 30, 2026 in [Science](https://news.mit.edu/2026/hidden-structure-behind-widely-used-class-materials-0430), and found that the tiny polarized regions believed to underpin the material's energy-storage and sensing behavior are smaller and more intricately correlated than leading simulations had predicted.

## What We Know

The study, titled "Bridging experiment and theory of relaxor ferroelectrics with multislice electron ptychography," was published in [Science](https://phys.org/news/2026-04-hidden-3d-atomic-relaxor-ferroelectrics.html) with DOI 10.1126/science.ads6023, and appears in volume 392, issue 6797, page 519 according to [ScienceDaily](https://www.sciencedaily.com/releases/2026/05/260504023831.htm). The senior author is James LeBeau, MIT's Kyocera Professor of Materials Science and Engineering. The co-first authors are MIT postdocs Michael Xu, who completed his PhD at MIT in 2025, and Menglin Zhu, as listed by [MIT News](https://news.mit.edu/2026/hidden-structure-behind-widely-used-class-materials-0430).

The sample studied was a lead magnesium niobate-lead titanate alloy, abbreviated PMN-PT, the workhorse compound of the relaxor ferroelectric family. The technique used was multi-slice electron ptychography, or MEP. As Zhu described it to [EurekAlert!](https://www.eurekalert.org/news-releases/1125896), the method scans a nanoscale electron probe across the sample so that successive measurements share a common region: "That creates regions of overlap, and that overlap has enough information to use an algorithm to iteratively reconstruct three-dimensional information."

The headline experimental result is that the polar regions inside PMN-PT are substantially smaller than the regions predicted by the leading molecular-dynamics models, and that the orientations of those regions are correlated with the surrounding chemical disorder rather than scattered at random. "Previously, these models basically had random regions of polarization, but they didn't tell you how those regions correlate with each other," Xu told [MIT News](https://news.mit.edu/2026/hidden-structure-behind-widely-used-class-materials-0430). In a separate quote carried by [Phys.org](https://phys.org/news/2026-04-hidden-3d-atomic-relaxor-ferroelectrics.html), Xu added: "Now we can tell you that information, and we can see how individual chemical species modulate polarization depending on the charge state of atoms."

The co-authors note that the discrepancy between experiment and simulation traces back to assumptions baked into earlier models. "We realized the chemical disorder we observed in our experiments was not fully considered previously," Xu and Zhu said in a joint statement reported by [EurekAlert!](https://www.eurekalert.org/news-releases/1125896). LeBeau framed the broader stakes for materials design to [Interesting Engineering](https://interestingengineering.com/science/mit-unveil-first-ever-3d-atomic-charge-map): "Now that we have a better understanding of exactly what's going on, we can better predict and engineer the properties we want materials to achieve."

The full author list spans five institutions. Beyond LeBeau, Xu, and Zhu, [MIT News](https://news.mit.edu/2026/hidden-structure-behind-widely-used-class-materials-0430) lists MIT PhD students Colin Gilgenbach and Bridget R. Denzer, Yubo Qi of the University of Alabama at Birmingham, Jieun Kim of the Korea Advanced Institute of Science and Technology, former University of Pennsylvania PhD student Jiahao Zhang, Lane W. Martin of Rice University, and Andrew M. Rappe of the University of Pennsylvania.

## Why It Matters

Relaxor ferroelectrics sit inside a wide range of devices that translate between electrical signals and mechanical motion or pressure. According to [MIT News](https://news.mit.edu/2026/hidden-structure-behind-widely-used-class-materials-0430), applications include ultrasound devices, microphones, sonar systems, sensors and actuators, defense systems, memory storage technologies, and energy-storage components. Despite that ubiquity, designers have historically had to tune the materials largely through trial and error because the link between their atomic-scale chemical disorder and their macroscopic response was not directly observable.

The MEP measurement closes part of that gap by letting experimentalists check, rather than infer, what the polar regions look like. As Xu told [ScienceDaily](https://www.sciencedaily.com/releases/2026/05/260504023831.htm), "This study is the first time in the electron microscope that we've been able to directly connect the three-dimensional polar structure of relaxor ferroelectrics with molecular dynamics calculations."

For simulation work, the result is a calibration target. The same models that have guided relaxor design for years can now be re-tuned against an experimental ground truth that includes the chemical disorder they previously approximated away. LeBeau described the cost of skipping that step in starker terms in remarks reported by [Interesting Engineering](https://interestingengineering.com/science/mit-unveil-first-ever-3d-atomic-charge-map), warning that without an experimental check on the inputs, materials simulations risk producing unreliable predictions.

## What We Don't Know

The paper is a structural measurement, not a device demonstration. The team has shown that the polar regions in PMN-PT are smaller and more correlated than expected, but it has not yet shown a new device built on that knowledge. None of the cited reporting from [MIT News](https://news.mit.edu/2026/hidden-structure-behind-widely-used-class-materials-0430), [Phys.org](https://phys.org/news/2026-04-hidden-3d-atomic-relaxor-ferroelectrics.html), [ScienceDaily](https://www.sciencedaily.com/releases/2026/05/260504023831.htm), [Interesting Engineering](https://interestingengineering.com/science/mit-unveil-first-ever-3d-atomic-charge-map), or [EurekAlert!](https://www.eurekalert.org/news-releases/1125896) quantifies how much the structural correction will move predicted figures of merit such as piezoelectric coefficients, dielectric constants, or energy density for any specific application. Whether the new structural picture transfers to other relaxor families beyond PMN-PT is also an open question that the cited reporting does not address.
